title: Limit Theorems for the Möbius function Function and Statistical Mechanics
speaker: Francesco Cellarosi
abstract: I will present a recent joint work with Ya.G. Sinai. We investigate the ``randomness" of the classical Möbius function by means of a statistical mechanical model for square-free numbers and we prove some new results, including a non-standard limit theorem where the Dickman-De Bruijn distribution appears. Although we use a probabilistic approach, this work is inspired by a conjecture by P. Sarnak, and by a number of recent results relating Number Theory and Ergodic Theory.

proof_stages.label: Möbius randomness and deterministic-test orthogonality
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the Möbius function, its cancellation links with the prime number theorem and Riemann hypothesis, and Sarnak's zero-entropy Möbius disjointness principle as the motivating randomness problem.

proof_stages.label: Sparse square-free prime ensemble Ω_m
proof_stages.summary: The natural interval is replaced by the set Ω_m of products of the first m primes, whose size, sparseness, inclusion of the usual square-free ensemble, and strong symmetries make basic Möbius sums exactly vanish.

proof_stages.label: Weighted probability ensemble and entropy scaling
proof_stages.summary: A probability measure π_m(n) proportional to 1/n is put on Ω_m, yielding independent non-identically distributed Bernoulli exponents ν_j and motivating the normalization of log n by log p_m through partition-function and entropy computations.

proof_stages.label: Limit law for the normalized logarithm
proof_stages.summary: For ζ_m=(∑ν_j log p_j)/log p_m the main limit theorem gives convergence in distribution to a law with explicit characteristic function exp∫_0^1(e^{iλv}-1)dv/v, identified with the Dickman--de Bruijn density after a Fourier/Riemann-sum proof.

proof_stages.label: Classical Dickman comparison and the role of weighting
proof_stages.summary: The speaker compares the limiting law with the classical smooth-number regimes of Dickman, de Bruijn, Hildebrand, and Erdős, explaining that the 1/n weights recover Dickman behavior in a regime where unweighted counting would not.

proof_stages.label: Shrinking-interval averaged error estimate
proof_stages.summary: Fixed-interval convergence is refined by chopping [0,1] into K shrinking subintervals, defining bin errors, and proving an averaged error bound for growing K while noting that a true local limit theorem for shrinking intervals is still missing.

proof_stages.label: Möbius cancellation for rescaled test functions
proof_stages.summary: Returning to Möbius randomness, the talk proves exact cancellation for one-periodic functions of log n/log p_m over Ω_m and an exponential-saving bound for long-period character tests, motivated by possible approximation of the original small ensemble.

proof_stages.label: Prime-factor fluctuations and ensemble compatibility
proof_stages.summary: Additional probabilistic refinements describe the statistics of ∑ν_j, including a central limit theorem and Poisson tail limits, to quantify how the measures on growing ensembles and subensembles fit together.

examples.name: Finite dynamical systems: PNT and Dirichlet cases
examples.note: Taking the topological system to be finite makes the Möbius orthogonality statement reduce to the prime number theorem or Dirichlet-type cancellation, illustrating basic known zero-entropy cases.

examples.name: Circle rotations and Davenport sums

examples.name: Smooth numbers and the Dickman--de Bruijn function
examples.note: The classical counting function Ψ(x,y) for y-smooth integers is recalled to show another native number-theoretic occurrence of the same Dickman--de Bruijn law and to contrast counting with the talk's 1/n weighting.
examples.engagement_reason: the Dickman–de Bruijn/smooth-number example is developed by defining the counting function, comparing asymptotic regimes, and computing how the speaker’s weighted ensemble recovers the Dickman distribution

passes.reason: Smooth numbers form the small-largest-prime-factor tail, and the y=log x regime is cited to show Dickman asymptotics cannot extend that far.
